must: Development = improving quality of life, not just money.
Three dimensions: economic, social and environmental.
GDP per capita = average economic output or income per person; HDI = income/GNI + education + life expectancy; Gini = inequality.
LIC incomes can be low because of low-value farming/raw materials, weak infrastructure, conflict and poor governance.
Countries earn money through exports: crops, minerals, manufactured goods, tourism, education and services.
gold: Development is a journey, not a label.
GDP shows money, but HDI shows a wider picture of quality of life.
Averages can hide inequality inside a country.
Raw materials usually earn less than manufactured goods, so LICs need value-added exports.
data: NZ inequality example: richest 10% own 59% of wealth; poorest 50% own 2%.
Use NZ vs Ethiopia to compare development pathway, indicators and DTM stage.
samples: HDI is better than GDP because it includes health and education, but it can still hide inequality because it uses national averages.
LIC income may be low when countries rely on raw materials with low unstable prices and lack infrastructure to process them into higher-value goods.
likely: Define development and dimensions.
Why HDI is better than GDP but still limited.
Why LIC income is low and how exports help.
Explain physical and human factors affecting development.

must: Food security = physical and economic access to enough safe and nutritious food.
Availability = food exists; access = people can afford/reach it; utilisation = food is safe and nutritious.
Sahel: low rainfall + overgrazing → bare soil → erosion → desertification → less food.
Yemen: conflict damages farms, roads, ports and markets, raising prices and causing malnutrition.
Sudan/South Sudan: conflict + climate shocks cause displacement, failed harvests and food insecurity.
gold: Food shortage answers need causes, consequences and solutions.
Conflict disrupts the whole food system: production, transport, markets and aid.
Technology helps production, but cannot solve hunger without access, peace and affordability.
data: Yemen: conflict since 2015 is a key cause of food insecurity.
Use Sahel as the physical-human interaction case.
Use Sudan/South Sudan as the second food case for breadth.
samples: In Yemen, conflict damages ports and roads, reducing food supply and increasing prices. This causes malnutrition and disease, lowering quality of life.
In the Sahel, low rainfall reduces vegetation and overgrazing removes remaining plants, causing erosion and desertification.
likely: Use Yemen to explain why conflict causes food shortages.
Climate vs conflict: to what extent?
Causes, impacts and responses for Sahel/Yemen/Sudan.

must: Physical water scarcity = not enough freshwater exists naturally.
Economic water scarcity = water exists but people lack money, pipes, pumps or infrastructure.
Clean water reduces disease, improves school/work attendance and supports farming.
GERD: Ethiopia wants electricity and development; Egypt fears reduced Nile water.
Aral Sea: river diversion for cotton irrigation caused shrinking, salty soils, fishing collapse and health issues.
gold: Water supports development through health, education, farming, income and quality of life.
Water can cause conflict, but also cooperation through data sharing, agreed releases and negotiation.
Always link water access to disease, school, work or food security.
data: Water access table examples: Norway 100%, New Zealand 95.6%, Brazil 73.1%, India 62.0%, Nigeria 23.7%, Ethiopia 12.0%.
GERD is on the Blue Nile in Ethiopia; Egypt is downstream.
samples: Clean water reduces cholera and diarrhoea, so children miss fewer school days and adults can work more productively.
GERD creates tension because Ethiopia benefits from hydroelectric power while Egypt fears less water for farming.
likely: Physical vs economic water scarcity.
How water improves development.
GERD conflict/cooperation.
Use water data to describe a pattern.

must: Export = good/service sold to another country; import = bought from another country.
Trade can create jobs, export income and tax revenue.
Trade can increase inequality if wages are low, factories unsafe or LICs export only raw materials.
Rana Plaza: Bangladesh garment factory complex collapsed in 2013; 1,134 killed and 2,500+ injured.
DRC/raw materials: resource-rich countries can stay poor if value is captured elsewhere.
gold: Trade helps development only when jobs are safe, wages fair and profits shared more equally.
Raw materials earn less than manufactured goods, so value-added exports matter.
Fast fashion shows that cheap goods can hide human and environmental costs.
data: Post-Rana Plaza inspections and Accord reforms show progress, but worker pay and power remain issues.
samples: Rana Plaza shows that global trade can create jobs but also expose workers to unsafe conditions when brands demand cheap clothing.
Raw-material exports can keep LICs poorer because prices are low/unstable and richer countries capture profit through manufacturing.
likely: Trade always helps development: to what extent?
Use Rana Plaza to explain progress and remaining inequality.
Why raw-material exports keep LICs poorer.
How countries earn money through exports.

must: Health and development are linked: illness reduces school, work, income and quality of life.
LICs often face more communicable diseases; HICs often face more non-communicable diseases because people live longer.
Malaria is spread by female Anopheles mosquitoes.
Malaria is common in warm, wet areas with stagnant water, poverty, poor housing and limited healthcare.
Responses: bed nets, indoor spraying, rapid tests, ACT medicines, vaccines, drainage and education.
gold: Malaria creates a poverty cycle: poor health lowers income, and poverty limits prevention and treatment.
For malaria, always include causes, consequences and solutions.
Health patterns reflect development level and healthcare access.
data: WHO-style global pattern: most malaria burden is in the African Region/Sub-Saharan Africa.
Under-5 children and pregnant women are especially vulnerable.
samples: Malaria slows development because children miss school, adults miss work and families spend money on treatment, reducing productivity and income.
No single malaria solution is enough because mosquitoes, poverty, housing and healthcare all affect transmission.
likely: Why malaria is common in Sub-Saharan Africa.
Consequences of malaria for development.
Evaluate malaria responses.
Compare LIC and HIC health patterns.

must: PEEL = Point, Evidence, Explanation, Link.
Describe = what you see; explain = why; evaluate/to what extent = both sides + judgement.
Source answers need exact data, place names and pattern/trend language.
Limitations: one year only, national averages, missing groups/causes/regional detail.
Every long answer must end with a development link.
gold: Never write only ‘it is high’; use numbers and places.
A strong link mentions health, education, income, jobs, equality or quality of life.
data: Good source phrase: ‘The highest value is… while the lowest is… showing a clear gap.’
Good limitation phrase: ‘This is a national average, so it may hide regional or wealth differences.’
samples: Point: Clean water improves development. Evidence: Ethiopia has much lower water access than Norway. Explanation: unsafe water increases disease and school absence. Link: therefore water affects health, education and quality of life.
For ‘to what extent’, write: I partly agree… however… therefore…
likely: Write a PEEL paragraph using a case study.
Use source data to describe a pattern.
Suggest limitations of a source.
Improve a weak answer.
